title: Dance
intro: Dance questions in quizbowl center overwhelmingly on classical ballet, both the 19th-century Russian Imperial repertoire and the choreographers who built it. Swan Lake, The Nutcracker, The Sleeping Beauty, Giselle, Coppelia, and La Bayadere anchor the canon, with Marius Petipa as the dominant choreographer and Tchaikovsky and Delibes as the recurring composers. A second major cluster covers Sergei Diaghilev's Ballets Russes, whose commissions (Stravinsky's The Firebird, Petrushka, and The Rite of Spring) and dancers (Nijinsky, Fokine, Massine) reshaped early-20th-century dance and supply some of the unit's densest cross-references between music and dance.

American dance forms the next largest cluster, split between ballet institution-builders (Balanchine and New York City Ballet, Jerome Robbins, Agnes de Mille) and modern dance founders who rejected ballet's vocabulary entirely — Martha Graham and Merce Cunningham foremost among them, with Alvin Ailey's Revelations and Twyla Tharp's crossover work extending the modern lineage into the postwar era. Individual star dancers recur as answerlines independent of any single company: Nureyev, Baryshnikov, Fonteyn, Pavlova, and Misty Copeland are tested on biography and technique (arabesque, pas de deux, en pointe, fouettes) as often as on any specific ballet.

A smaller but consistent share of questions covers dance outside the ballet-modern axis: musical theater and film choreography (Bob Fosse, Fred Astaire, tap dancing), and social and folk traditions from around the world (tango, flamenco, belly dance, lindy hop, breakdancing, square dance). Countries and cities appear as organizing answerlines in their own right — Russia, France, Denmark, and New York City each gather clues about the companies and traditions associated with them rather than a single work or person.

sections.name: Classical and Romantic Ballet
sections.blurb: The 19th-century story-ballet canon and the composers who scored it.

entries.note: Tchaikovsky ballet of Odette and her evil double Odile, cursed by the sorcerer Rothbart; choreographed by Petipa and Ivanov.
French-Russian choreographer of the Imperial Ballet who codified the classical repertoire: Swan Lake, Sleeping Beauty, and Nutcracker.
Tchaikovsky's Christmas ballet of Clara, the Sugar Plum Fairy, and the Nutcracker Prince; choreographed by Petipa and Ivanov.
Romantic ballet of a peasant girl who dies of heartbreak and becomes a Wili; choreographed by Coralli and Perrot.
Tchaikovsky ballet of Princess Aurora, cursed by Carabosse and awakened by a prince's kiss; choreographed by Petipa.
Delibes's comic ballet about a lifelike dancing doll built by the toymaker Dr. Coppelius.
Petipa's Orientalist ballet set in India, famous for its "Kingdom of the Shades" corps de ballet scene.
French Romantic composer of the ballets Coppelia and Sylvia.
Minkus's comic ballet loosely based on Cervantes, famous for its show-stopping Kitri and Basilio grand pas de deux.
Romantic ballet of a peasant girl who dies of heartbreak and becomes a Wili; choreographed by Coralli and Perrot.
The Nutcracker's regal fairy ruler of the Land of Sweets, famous for her celesta-scored variation.
Refers to Marius Petipa, French-Russian choreographer who codified the classical ballet repertoire.

sections.name: The Ballets Russes Era
sections.blurb: Diaghilev's early-20th-century company and the composers, dancers, and scandals it produced.

entries.note: Stravinsky's 1913 ballet of pagan sacrifice whose premiere provoked a riot; choreographed by Nijinsky for the Ballets Russes.
Legendary Ballets Russes dancer-choreographer of The Rite of Spring and Afternoon of a Faun; Diaghilev's star performer.
Choreographer for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes who created The Firebird and The Dying Swan.
Debussy-scored Ballets Russes work choreographed and danced by Nijinsky, controversial for its sensual final gesture.
Fokine's short solo set to Saint-Saens's music, famously performed by Anna Pavlova.
Stravinsky's first Ballets Russes score, based on Russian folklore about a magical bird; choreographed by Fokine.
Russian composer of the Ballets Russes scores The Firebird, Petrushka, and The Rite of Spring.
Ballets Russes dancer-choreographer who created Parade and later worked in symphonic ballet.
Ballets Russes work with music by Satie, scenario by Cocteau, sets by Picasso, and choreography by Massine.
Russian impresario who founded the Ballets Russes, commissioning Stravinsky and employing Nijinsky and Fokine.

sections.name: 20th-Century Ballet Companies and Choreographers
sections.blurb: Balanchine, Ashton, Robbins, and the national companies that shaped modern ballet repertoire.

entries.note: Russian-American choreographer who co-founded New York City Ballet and pioneered neoclassical, plotless ballet.
Prokofiev's ballet adaptation of Shakespeare's tragedy, a staple 20th-century repertory work choreographed by Lavrovsky and others.
British choreographer and founding figure of the Royal Ballet, known for narrative works like Cinderella.
Britain's national ballet company, based at Covent Garden, shaped by founding choreographer Frederick Ashton.
American choreographer who integrated ballet into Broadway with Oklahoma!'s dream ballet; also choreographed Rodeo.
American choreographer at New York City Ballet and on Broadway, choreographing West Side Story.
American ballet company founded by Robert Joffrey, known for reviving The Rite of Spring's original choreography.
First major Native American prima ballerina, a leading dancer for Balanchine's New York City Ballet.
Agnes de Mille's Copland-scored ballet about a tomboyish cowgirl finding romance at a ranch.

sections.name: Modern Dance Pioneers
sections.blurb: The early modern dance rebellion against ballet convention, led by Graham and Cunningham.

entries.note: American modern dance pioneer who developed the contraction-and-release technique bearing her name; choreographed Appalachian Spring.
American modern dance choreographer who used chance procedures and long collaborated with composer John Cage.
Martha Graham's modern dance work set to Aaron Copland's score, depicting American pioneer life.

sections.name: Concert Dance and Companies
sections.blurb: Postwar modern and contemporary dance companies built around a single choreographer's vision.

entries.note: American choreographer who founded the Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater; known for the modern dance suite Revelations.
Alvin Ailey's signature modern dance suite set to spirituals and blues, exploring African-American religious experience.
American choreographer blending ballet and modern dance with popular music; choreographed Movin' Out.
American dancer-anthropologist who fused Caribbean and African dance forms into modern dance technique.
American choreographer who founded the Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater; known for the modern dance suite Revelations.

sections.name: Ballet Stars and Technique
sections.blurb: Celebrated dancers and the vocabulary of positions, steps, and training methods they embody.

entries.note: Ballet technique of dancing on the tips of the toes, enabled by reinforced pointe shoes.
Ballet position with one leg extended straight behind the body while balancing on the other.
American Ballet Theatre principal, the first Black woman to hold that rank in the company's history.
Swan Lake's Black Swan, the sorcerer Rothbart's daughter who deceives Prince Siegfried; famous for her 32 fouettes.
Ballet duet, typically for a ballerina and male partner, forming the centerpiece of classical story ballets.
Soviet-born dancer who defected to the West in 1961; renowned partner of Margot Fonteyn.
Russian dancer and pedagogue whose codified training method, the Vaganova method, is taught worldwide.
Russian ballerina famed for her performance of The Dying Swan and worldwide touring company.
Elevation movements like the grand jete and entrechat that showcase a dancer's technique.
British prima ballerina and longtime Royal Ballet star, celebrated for her partnership with Rudolf Nureyev.
Soviet-born dancer who defected in 1974; noted for American Ballet Theatre roles and later modern dance work.

sections.name: Musical Theater and Popular Dance
sections.blurb: Dance on the Broadway stage and in Hollywood film musicals.

entries.note: American choreographer of angular, jazz-hands style; choreographed Chicago and Cabaret.
American film dancer known for elegant tap and ballroom routines, frequent partner of Ginger Rogers.
American dance style using metal-tipped shoes to create rhythmic percussion, popularized by Fred Astaire.

sections.name: Social, Folk, and World Dance
sections.blurb: Vernacular partnered and community dance traditions outside the concert stage.

entries.note: Sensual partnered dance originating in Argentina and Uruguay's Rio de la Plata region.
Middle Eastern dance style emphasizing hip and torso articulation, associated with Egyptian and Turkish traditions.
Acrobatic street dance style originating in the 1970s New York hip-hop scene.
Passionate Andalusian dance style combining percussive footwork, guitar, and cante singing.
Energetic partnered swing dance that emerged in 1920s-30s Harlem alongside big-band jazz.
American folk dance for four couples in a square formation, guided by a caller.

sections.name: Countries, Terms, and Motifs
sections.blurb: Nations, companies, and general vocabulary that recur across dance styles and eras.

entries.note: Home to George Balanchine's New York City Ballet and American Ballet Theatre.
Home to the Imperial Ballet, the Ballets Russes, and choreographers Petipa, Fokine, and Balanchine.
Home to American Ballet Theatre, New York City Ballet, and modern dance pioneers Graham and Cunningham.
Color associated with the traditional Romantic tutu and Odette's virtuous swan in Swan Lake.
General Western theatrical dance form built on codified positions and technique, developed from French and Russian court traditions.
Home to the Royal Danish Ballet and the Bournonville technique.
Birthplace of merengue and bachata, popular partnered social dances.
Percussion instruments central to many folk and world dance traditions' rhythmic accompaniment.
Birthplace of codified ballet technique under Louis XIV and home to the Paris Opera Ballet.
Home of Irish step dance, popularized internationally by Riverdance.
Home to flamenco, the passionate Andalusian dance tradition.
